subject: Physics
q:
Given below are two statements :

Statement - I : Susceptibilities of paramagnetic and ferromagnetic substances increase with decrease in temperature.

Statement - II : Diamagnetism is a result of orbital motions of electrons developing magnetic moments opposite to the applied magnetic field.

Choose the correct answer from the options given below
question:
Given below are two statements :

Statement - I : Susceptibilities of paramagnetic and ferromagnetic substances increase with decrease in temperature.

Statement - II : Diamagnetism is a result of orbital motions of electrons developing magnetic moments opposite to the applied magnetic field.

Choose the correct answer from the options given below
options: Both Statement - I and Statement - II are true.
Both Statement - I and Statement - II are false.
Statement - I is true but Statement - II is false.
Statement- I is false but Statement - II is true.
solution:
Statement-I is true as susceptibility of ferromagnetic and paramagnetic materials is inversely related to temperature, so it increases with decrease in temperature.

Statement-II is also true as because of orbital motion of electrons, the diamagnetic material is able to oppose external magnetic field. Once an external magnetic field is applied, diamagnetic materials develop an induced magnetic field opposite to this field and thus are repelled by the field.

subject: Physics
q:
Given below are two statements:

Statement I: For diamagnetic substance -1≤x<0, where x is the magnetic susceptibility.

Statement II: Diamagnetic substance when placed in an external magnetic field, tend to move from stronger to weaker part of the field. In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options give below.
question:
Given below are two statements:

Statement I: For diamagnetic substance -1≤x<0, where x is the magnetic susceptibility.

Statement II: Diamagnetic substance when placed in an external magnetic field, tend to move from stronger to weaker part of the field. In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options give below.
options: Both Statement I and Statement II are False
Statement I is correct but Statement II is false
Statement I is incorrect but Statement II is true
Both Statement I and Statement II are true
solution:
There are no atomic dipoles in diamagnetic materials because the resultant magnetic moment of each atom is zero due to paired electrons. Diamagnetic materials are repelled by a magnet. These substances are weakly repelled by the field, so in a nonuniform field, these substances have a tendency to move from a strong to a weak part of the external magnetic field.

For diamagnetic substance (-1<x<0) and a diamagnetic substance is repelled by the magnetic field.

q: A compass needle of oscillation magnetometer oscillates <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn></math> times per minute at a place <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> of dip <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><mo>°</mo></math>. The number of oscillations per minute become <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn></math> at another place <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Q</mi></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn><mo>°</mo></math> dip. The ratio of the total magnetic field at the two places <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><msub><mi>B</mi><mi>Q</mi></msub><mo>:</mo><msub><mi>B</mi><mi>P</mi></msub></mrow></mfenced></math> is:
question: A compass needle of oscillation magnetometer oscillates <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn></math> times per minute at a place <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> of dip <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><mo>°</mo></math>. The number of oscillations per minute become <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn></math> at another place <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Q</mi></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn><mo>°</mo></math> dip. The ratio of the total magnetic field at the two places <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><msub><mi>B</mi><mi>Q</mi></msub><mo>:</mo><msub><mi>B</mi><mi>P</mi></msub></mrow></mfenced></math> is:
solution:
At point P the needle oscillates 20 times per minute. Therefore, time period TP=6020= 3 s and at point Q, TQ=6010=6 s.

The time period of oscillation magnetometer is given by,

T=2πIBHM

⇒TP=3=2πIBPcos30°M

And TQ=6 s=2πIBQcos60°M

Taking ratio,

36=1 BP32×BQ2

⇒34=BQBP

⇒BQ:BP=3:4

q: A ceiling fan having <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn></math> blades of length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>80</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> each is rotating with an angular velocity of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1200</mn><mo> </mo><mi>rpm</mi></math>. The magnetic field of earth in that region is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">G</mi></math> and angle of dip is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><mo>°</mo></math>. The emf induced across the blades is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mi>π</mi><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>5</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></math> is _______.
question: A ceiling fan having <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn></math> blades of length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>80</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> each is rotating with an angular velocity of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1200</mn><mo> </mo><mi>rpm</mi></math>. The magnetic field of earth in that region is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">G</mi></math> and angle of dip is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><mo>°</mo></math>. The emf induced across the blades is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mi>π</mi><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>5</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></math> is _______.
solution:
Component of the magnetic field perpendicular to the plane of fan blades, Bv=Bsin30°=0.5×10-4 T×12=14×10-4 T

Angular frequency, ω=2π×f=2π60×1200 rad s-1=40π rad s-1

Therefore, induced EMF

ε=12BVωl2=12×14×10-4×40π×0.82

=32π×10-5 V

subject: Physics
q:
Given below are two statements:

Statement I : The diamagnetic property depends on temperature.

Statement II : The induced magnetic dipole moment in a diamagnetic sample is always opposite to the magnetising field.

In the light of given statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below
question:
Given below are two statements:

Statement I : The diamagnetic property depends on temperature.

Statement II : The induced magnetic dipole moment in a diamagnetic sample is always opposite to the magnetising field.

In the light of given statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below
options: Both Statement I and Statement II are False
Statement I is incorrect but Statement II is true
Statement I is correct but Statement II is false
Both Statement I and Statement II are true
solution:
The properties of a diamagnetic material are as follows:

1. The susceptibility has a low negative value.

2. Susceptibility is independent of temperature.

3. The relative permeability is slightly less than one.

4. When placed in a non-uniform magnetic field they have a tendency to move away from the field.

5. When suspended freely in a uniform magnetic field, they set themselves perpendicular to the direction of the magnetic field.

Hence, Statement I is false, but statement II is true.

q: In a ferromagnetic material, below the curie temperature, a domain is defined as:
question: In a ferromagnetic material, below the curie temperature, a domain is defined as:
options: a macroscopic region with saturation magnetization.
a macroscopic region with consecutive magnetic dipoles oriented in opposite direction.
a macroscopic region with zero magnetization.
a macroscopic region with randomly oriented magnetic dipoles.
solution: In a ferromagnetic material, below the curie temperature, a domain is defined as a macroscopic region with saturation magnetization.

question: A compass needle oscillates 20 times per minute at a place where the dip is 30o and 30 times per minute where the dip is 60o. The ratio of total magnetic field due to the earth at two places respectively is 4x. The value of x is
solution:
The formula for time period is given by 

T=2πIMBH

⇒T=2πIMBcosθ

Let

 θ1=30°θ2=60°

The ratio of the time periods is,

T1T2=60206030=B2cos60°B1cos30°=B2B1×1232

⇒B1B2=4243

question: A magnetic compass needle oscillates <math><mn>30</mn></math> times per minute at a place where the dip is <math><mn>45</mn><mo>°</mo></math> and <math><mn>40</mn></math> times per minute where the dip is <math><mn>30</mn><mo>°</mo></math>. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>B</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>B</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> are the net magnetic fields due to the earth at the two places respectively, then the ratio <math><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub><mo>/</mo><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></math> is approximately equal to
solution:
Let f1 and f2 be the respective oscillation frequencies respectively for 45° and 30° dip. Then,

f1=12πMB1cos45°I

f2=12πMB2cos30°I

f1f2=B1cos45°B2cos30°

⇒302402=B1cos45°B2cos30° 

⇒B1B2=900×cos30°1600×cos45°

⇒B1B2=0.688≈0.7

Which of the following statements are correct?
(A) Electric monopoles do not exist whereas magnetic monopoles exist.
(B) Magnetic field lines due to a solenoid at its ends and outside cannot be completely straight and confined.
(C) Magnetic field lines are completely confined within a toroid.
(D) Magnetic field lines inside a bar magnet are not parallel.
(E) χ=-1 is the condition for a perfect diamagnetic material, where χ is its magnetic susceptibility.

Choose the correct answer from the options given below :
question:
Which of the following statements are correct?
(A) Electric monopoles do not exist whereas magnetic monopoles exist.
(B) Magnetic field lines due to a solenoid at its ends and outside cannot be completely straight and confined.
(C) Magnetic field lines are completely confined within a toroid.
(D) Magnetic field lines inside a bar magnet are not parallel.
(E) χ=-1 is the condition for a perfect diamagnetic material, where χ is its magnetic susceptibility.

Choose the correct answer from the options given below :
solution:
Statement (C) is correct because, the magnetic field outside the toroid is zero and they form closed loops inside the toroid itself.

Statement (E) is correct because we know that super conductors are materials inside which the net magnetic field is always zero and they are perfect diamagnetic.

μr=1+χ

χ=-1

μr=0

For superconductors.

q: The percentage increase in magnetic field (B) when space within a current carrying solenoid is filled with magnesium (magnetic susceptibility $\left.\chi_{\mathrm{mg}}=1.2 \times 10^{-5}\right)$ is :
question: The percentage increase in magnetic field (B) when space within a current carrying solenoid is filled with magnesium (magnetic susceptibility $\left.\chi_{\mathrm{mg}}=1.2 \times 10^{-5}\right)$ is :
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \% \text { change in } B=\frac{B_{\text {new }}-B_{\text {old }}}{B_{\text {old }}} \times 100 \% \\ & =\frac{\mu \mathrm{ni}-\mu_0 \mathrm{ni}}{\mu_0 \mathrm{ni}} \times 100 \%=\frac{\left(\mu-\mu_0\right)}{\mu_0} \times 100 \% \\ & =\frac{\left(\mu_0 \mu_{\mathrm{r}}-\mu_0\right)}{\mu_0} \times 100 \% \\ & =\left(\mu_{\mathrm{r}}-1\right) \times 100 \% \\ & =\chi_{\mathrm{n}} \times 100 \% \\ & =1.2 \times 10^{-3} \%\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: A bar magnet is released from rest along the axis of a very long vertical copper tube. After some time the magnet will
question: A bar magnet is released from rest along the axis of a very long vertical copper tube. After some time the magnet will
options: Move down with an acceleration equal to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math>
Move down with almost constant speed
Move down with an acceleration greater than <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math>
solution:
The flux connected to the copper tube will fluctuate when the magnet moves under the influence of gravity. Eddy currents will be created in the copper tube's body as a result, these induced currents oppose the collapse of the magnet, in accordance with Lenz's law.

A retarding force will consequently act on the magnet. This force will keep rising with the magnet's rising velocity until it equals the force of gravity. The net force acting on the magnet will then vanish. The magnet will therefore reach a steady speed.

q: At some location  the horizontal component of earth's magnetic field is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>18</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>6</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi><mo>.</mo></math> At this location, magnetic needle of length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0.12</mn><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> and pole strength <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1.8</mn><mo> </mo><mi>Am</mi></math> is suspended from its mid-point using a thread, it makes <math><mn>45</mn><mo>°</mo></math> angles with horizontal in equilibrium. To keep this needle horizontal, the vertical force that should be applied at one of its ends is:
question: At some location  the horizontal component of earth's magnetic field is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>18</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>6</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi><mo>.</mo></math> At this location, magnetic needle of length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0.12</mn><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> and pole strength <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1.8</mn><mo> </mo><mi>Am</mi></math> is suspended from its mid-point using a thread, it makes <math><mn>45</mn><mo>°</mo></math> angles with horizontal in equilibrium. To keep this needle horizontal, the vertical force that should be applied at one of its ends is:
solution:
The horizontal and vertical components of earth's magnetic field (BH and BV) are related as

BVBH=tanθ

Here, θ=45° and BH=18×10-6 T

⇒BV=BHtan45°

⇒BV=BH=18×10-6 T   ∵ tan45°=1

Now, when the external force F is applied, to keep the needle stays in horizontal position is shown below,

Taking torque at point P, we get

mBV×2l=Fl

∴ F=2×mBV

Substituting the given values, we get

F=2×1.8×18×10-6

F=6.48×10-5=6.5×10-5 N.

subject: Physics
q:
Given below are two statements: one is labelled as Assertion (A) and the other is labelled as Reason (R).
Consider a ferromagnetic material :
Assertion (A) : The individual atoms in a ferromagnetic material possess a magnetic dipole moment and interact with one another in such a way that they spontaneously align themselves forming domains.
Reason (R): At high enough temperature, the domain structure of ferromagnetic material disintegrates. Thus, magnetization will disappear at high enough temperature known as Curie temperature.
In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below :
question:
Given below are two statements: one is labelled as Assertion (A) and the other is labelled as Reason (R).
Consider a ferromagnetic material :
Assertion (A) : The individual atoms in a ferromagnetic material possess a magnetic dipole moment and interact with one another in such a way that they spontaneously align themselves forming domains.
Reason (R): At high enough temperature, the domain structure of ferromagnetic material disintegrates. Thus, magnetization will disappear at high enough temperature known as Curie temperature.
In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below :
options: Both (A) and (R) are true but (R) is not the correct explanation of (A)
Both (A) and (R) are true and (R) is the correct explanation of (A)
solution:
Assertion (A) states that individual atoms in a ferromagnetic material possess a magnetic dipole moment and interact to form domains. This is a correct description of the domain theory of ferromagnetism, where exchange interactions lead to spontaneous alignment of moments within small regions called domains.
Reason (R) states that at high temperatures (above the Curie temperature $T_C$), the domain structure disintegrates due to thermal agitation, and the material becomes paramagnetic. This is also a correct statement regarding the temperature dependence of ferromagnetic materials.
However, Reason (R) describes the effect of temperature on the domain structure, whereas Assertion (A) describes the fundamental nature and formation of domains in the ferromagnetic state. The disintegration of domains at high temperature is not the reason why they form spontaneously at lower temperatures. Therefore, both statements are true, but (R) is not the correct explanation of (A).

subject: Physics
q: A magnet of total magnetic moment <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math> is placed in a time varying magnetic field, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi><mover accent="true"><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>cos</mi><mi>ω</mi><mi>t</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> where <math><mi>B</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn></math> Tesla and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>ω</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0.125</mn><mi> </mi><mi>rad</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. The work done for reversing the direction of the magnetic moment at <math><mi>t</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn></math> second, is:
question: A magnet of total magnetic moment <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math> is placed in a time varying magnetic field, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi><mover accent="true"><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>cos</mi><mi>ω</mi><mi>t</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> where <math><mi>B</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn></math> Tesla and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>ω</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0.125</mn><mi> </mi><mi>rad</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. The work done for reversing the direction of the magnetic moment at <math><mi>t</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn></math> second, is:
solution:
As we know that, work done in rotating a magnetic dipole in magnetic field is,

W=MB(cosθ1-cosθ2)    ...(1)

Now in the above given question we have, Magnetic Moment (M)=10-2 i^ A m2,Magnetic Field=Bi^cosωt with B=1 T , ω=0.125 rad s-1 and t=1 s

Let initial angle (θ1)=0∘ so on reversing the direction of magnetic moment final angle (θ2)=180∘

Now, substituting all the values in equation (1) we get,

⇒W=10-2×(1)cos0.125×1cos0∘-cos180∘

⇒W=10-2cos0.1251--1   (∵cos0∘=1 and cos180∘=-1)

⇒W=2×10-2×0.99

⇒W≈0.02 J

Therefore, the work done in reversing the direction of the magnetic moment is 0.02 J.

subject: Physics
q: The space inside a straight current carrying solenoid is filled with a magnetic material having magnetic susceptibility equal to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>5</mn></mrow></msup></math>. What is fractional increase in the magnetic field inside solenoid with respect to air as medium inside the solenoid?
question: The space inside a straight current carrying solenoid is filled with a magnetic material having magnetic susceptibility equal to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>5</mn></mrow></msup></math>. What is fractional increase in the magnetic field inside solenoid with respect to air as medium inside the solenoid?
solution:
Given here, χ=1.2×10-5

Magnetic field inside the material in solenoid is B'=μ01+χni, here, i is current.

Now, magnetic field with air as medium inside solenoid is B=μ0ni.

So, fractional increase is B'-BB=χ=1.2×10-5

subject: Physics
q: A magnetic needle lying parallel to a magnetic field requires $\mathrm{W}$ units of work to turn it through $60^{\circ}$. The torque needed to maintain the needle in this position will be
question: A magnetic needle lying parallel to a magnetic field requires $\mathrm{W}$ units of work to turn it through $60^{\circ}$. The torque needed to maintain the needle in this position will be
solution: $\tau=(\mathrm{H}) \tan 60^{\circ} \quad=\mathrm{W} \cdot \sqrt{3}$

subject: Physics
q:
Given below are two statements: one is labelled as
Assertion A and the other is labelled as Reason R
Assertion A: A bar magnet dropped through a metallic cylindrical pipe takes more time to come down compared to a non-magnetic bar with same geometry and mass.
Reason R: For the magnetic bar, Eddy currents are produced in the metallic pipe which oppose the motion of the magnetic bar.
In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below
question:
Given below are two statements: one is labelled as
Assertion A and the other is labelled as Reason R
Assertion A: A bar magnet dropped through a metallic cylindrical pipe takes more time to come down compared to a non-magnetic bar with same geometry and mass.
Reason R: For the magnetic bar, Eddy currents are produced in the metallic pipe which oppose the motion of the magnetic bar.
In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below
options: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="bold">A</mi></math> is false but <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="bold">R</mi></math> is true

Both <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="bold">A</mi></math> and R are true and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="bold">R</mi></math> is the correct explanation of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="bold">A</mi></math>
<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="bold">A</mi></math> is true but <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="bold">R</mi></math> is false

Both <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="bold">A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="bold">R</mi></math> are true and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="bold">R</mi></math> is NOT the correct explanation of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="bold">A</mi></math>
solution:
Due to change in flux, eddy current is produced which opposes the motion in the metallic pipe for the bar magnet.

Eddy currents are loops of electrical current induced within conductors by a changing magnetic field in the conductor according to Faraday's law of induction. Eddy currents flow in closed loops within conductors, in planes perpendicular to the magnetic field.

Hence, R is the correct explanation and both statements are true.

subject: Physics
q: The soft-iron is a suitable material for making an electromagnet. This is because soft-iron has
question: The soft-iron is a suitable material for making an electromagnet. This is because soft-iron has
solution: Electromagnets are made of soft iron because of its high permeability and low retentivity. High permeability means that the core provides complete linkage of magnetic flux of the primary coil to the secondary coil.  The determination of the magnetism left in the material after the removal of the magnetizing field is known as retentivity. The required condition for the operation of electromagnets is the fast reversal of polarity, so high retentivity is undesirable. So, soft iron core has high permeability and low retentivity.

subject: Physics
q: The free space inside a current carrying toroid is filled with a material of susceptibility 2×10-2. The percentage increase in the value of magnetic field inside the toroid will be
question: The free space inside a current carrying toroid is filled with a material of susceptibility 2×10-2. The percentage increase in the value of magnetic field inside the toroid will be
solution:
It is given that 

χm=2×10-2

The permeability is μr=1+χm=1+0.02=1.02.

The magnetic field is B=μrB0=1.02B0

Hence, percentage increase in magnetic field is 

B-B0B0×100%=B01.02-1B0×100%=2%

subject: Physics
q: A bar magnet of length $6 \mathrm{~cm}$ has a magnetic moment of $4 \mathrm{~J} \mathrm{~T}^{-1}$. Find the strength of magnetic field at a distance of $200 \mathrm{~cm}$ from the centre of the magnet along its equatorial line.
question: A bar magnet of length $6 \mathrm{~cm}$ has a magnetic moment of $4 \mathrm{~J} \mathrm{~T}^{-1}$. Find the strength of magnetic field at a distance of $200 \mathrm{~cm}$ from the centre of the magnet along its equatorial line.
solution:
Along the equatorial line, magnetic field strength
$$
\left.B=\frac{\mu_0}{4 \pi} \frac{M}{\left(r^2+\ell^2{ }^{3 / 2}\right.}\right)
$$
Given: $M=4 \mathrm{~J} T^{-1}$
$$
r=200 \mathrm{~cm}=2 \mathrm{~m}
$$
$\ell=\frac{6 \mathrm{~cm}}{2}=3 \mathrm{~cm}=3 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{~m}$
$$
\therefore B=\frac{4 \pi \times 10^{-7}}{4 \pi} \times \frac{4}{\left[2^2+\left(3 \times 10^{-2}\right)^2\right]^{3 / 2}}
$$
Solving we get, $B=5 \times 10^{-8}$ tesla

subject: Physics
q: A metallic rod of length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> is placed in North-South direction and is moved at a constant speed of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> towards East. The horizontal component of the Earth's magnetic field at that place is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi></math> and the angle of dip is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>45</mn><mo>°</mo></math>. The emf induced in the rod is _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>mV</mi></math>.
question: A metallic rod of length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> is placed in North-South direction and is moved at a constant speed of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> towards East. The horizontal component of the Earth's magnetic field at that place is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi></math> and the angle of dip is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>45</mn><mo>°</mo></math>. The emf induced in the rod is _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>mV</mi></math>.
solution:
Emf produced is ε=BVL⊥v⊥

We know BVBH=tan45°, so BV=4×10-3 T

Then,  ε=4×10-3×20×10-2×20 =16 mV

subject: Physics
q: A magnet hung at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>45</mn><mo>°</mo></math> with magnetic meridian makes an angle of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn><mo>°</mo></math> with the horizontal. The actual value of the angle of dip is
question: A magnet hung at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>45</mn><mo>°</mo></math> with magnetic meridian makes an angle of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn><mo>°</mo></math> with the horizontal. The actual value of the angle of dip is
solution:
Given here, θ'=60° and apparent value of dip, α=45°.

If H is horizontal component of earth's magnetic field in magnetic meridian, then tanθ'=VH.

Let H1 be component of H at 60° to magnetic meridian, then tanθ=VH1=VHcosα=tanθ'cosα

tanθ=tan60°×cos45°

⇒tanθ=3×12

Thus, the actual value of the angle of dip is θ=tan-132

subject: Physics
q: The vertical component of the earth's magnetic field is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>5</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi></math> at any place where the angle of dip is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>37</mn><mo>°</mo></math>. The earth's resultant magnetic field at that place will be (Given <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>tan</mi><mn>37</mn><mo>°</mo><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mn>3</mn><mn>4</mn></mfrac></math>)
question: The vertical component of the earth's magnetic field is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>5</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi></math> at any place where the angle of dip is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>37</mn><mo>°</mo></math>. The earth's resultant magnetic field at that place will be (Given <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>tan</mi><mn>37</mn><mo>°</mo><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mn>3</mn><mn>4</mn></mfrac></math>)
solution:
Given here:

The vertical component of the earths magnetic field BV=6×10-5 T and angle of dip δ=37°.

For vertical component, BV=Bsinδ

Putting the values, we have 

⇒6×10-5=B35

Earth's magnetic field at that place as B=10×10-5 T =10-4 T

subject: Physics
q: The length of a magnet is large compared to its width and breadth. The time period of its width and breadth. The time period of its oscillation in a vibration magnetometer is $2 \mathrm{~s}$. The magnet is cut along its length into three equal parts and three parts are then placed on each other with their like poles together. The time period of this combination will be
question: The length of a magnet is large compared to its width and breadth. The time period of its width and breadth. The time period of its oscillation in a vibration magnetometer is $2 \mathrm{~s}$. The magnet is cut along its length into three equal parts and three parts are then placed on each other with their like poles together. The time period of this combination will be
solution:
Time period of vibration, $T=2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{1}{M B}}$
Where $\ell=$ moment of inertia of magnet, $\mathrm{M}=$ magnetic moment $\mathrm{I}=\frac{\mathrm{m} \ell^2}{12}$ and $\mathrm{M}=$ pole strength $\times \ell$
$$
I^{\prime}=\frac{1}{12}\left(\frac{m}{3}\right)\left(\frac{\ell}{3}\right)^2 \times 3=\frac{\mathrm{l}}{9}
$$
and $M^{\prime}=$ pole strength (will remain the same) $\times(\ell / 3) \times 3=M$.
$$
\mathrm{T}^{\prime}=\frac{\mathrm{T}}{\sqrt{9}}=\frac{2}{9} \mathrm{~s} .
$$

subject: Physics
q: A thin rectangular magnet suspended freely has a period of oscillation equal to $T$. Now it is broken into two equal halves (each having half of the original length) and one piece is made to oscillate freely in the same field. If its period of oscillation is $T^{\prime}$, the ratio $\frac{T^{\prime}}{T}$ is
question: A thin rectangular magnet suspended freely has a period of oscillation equal to $T$. Now it is broken into two equal halves (each having half of the original length) and one piece is made to oscillate freely in the same field. If its period of oscillation is $T^{\prime}$, the ratio $\frac{T^{\prime}}{T}$ is
solution:
$\mathrm{T} \propto \sqrt{1}$
$\frac{T_1}{T_2}=\sqrt{\frac{I_1}{I_2}} ; \quad \mathrm{I} \propto \mathrm{L}^2 \quad\left[\Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{I}_1}{\mathrm{I}_2}=\frac{\mathrm{L}_1^2}{\mathrm{~L}_2^2}=\frac{\left(2 \mathrm{~L}_2\right)^2}{\mathrm{~L}_2^2}=4\right] \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{T}_1}{\mathrm{~T}_2}=\sqrt{4}=2$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{T}_2=\frac{\mathrm{T}_1}{2} \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{T}_2}{\mathrm{~T}_1}=\frac{1}{2}$
